findings.description: touch() reads the session dict raw without purging or checking expires_at, so calling it on an expired-but-not-yet-purged token resurrects a dead session and returns True, contradicting get()'s expired=gone contract.
user_sessions() neither calls _purge_expired() nor filters on expires_at, so it returns expired-but-unpurged sessions, inconsistent with get() and active_count() which treat expiry as authoritative.
create() assigns self._sessions[token] without checking for an existing token, so a (astronomically rare) token collision silently overwrites and destroys the prior user's session.
ttl is accepted unvalidated; a zero or negative ttl makes every session expire immediately (get always returns None after purge) with no error raised.
Out of logic scope, noted in passing: _new_token() uses non-cryptographic random instead of secrets, making session tokens predictable — defer detail to critic-security.
